network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine primarily through literature-grounded cyanosulfidic Strecker chemistry centered on HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile, while integrating hydrothermal production/concentration of reduced carbon feedstocks and prebiotic capture of labile threonine into peptides. Ten pathways are defined by combining experimentally supported routes: (i) lactaldehyde-based Strecker synthesis, (ii) glyceraldehyde-based Strecker synthesis, (iii) Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin chemistry from lactaldehyde, (iv-v) spark-discharge source routes feeding the same selective downstream intermediates, (vi) low-selectivity but literature-supported HCN polymer hydrolysate access, (vii) conceptually plausible glycine-acetaldehyde aldol condensation explicitly marked speculative, (viii) ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy-amino-acid pool chemistry explicitly marked nonspecific/speculative for threonine assignment, (ix) α-keto-acid reductive amination explicitly marked extrapolative from demonstrated keto-acid amination studies, and (x) rapid biochemical peptide capture to mitigate threonine instability. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively to supply formate, acetate, and pyruvate and to provide concentration via FeS/greigite/magnetite vent systems, while target-forming steps are placed mainly in cooler surface settings consistent with the literature on threonine fragility.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; H2-rich, anoxic, serpentinization-fed CO2 reduction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases yields formate as an early fixed-carbon product.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite- and FeS-mediated CO2 reduction is central to alkaline vent carbon fixation models and provides a plausible hydrothermal feedstock without requiring unstable threonine formation at high temperature; vent mineralogy and low-temperature chimney-wall FeS/greigite formation are supported by White et al. (2015), and hydrothermal amino acid thermodynamics/concentration logic by Shock & Canovas (1998) and Lane & Martin (2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduction of formate to acetate

conditions.other: alkaline/subseafloor hydrothermal setting with H2 and CO2/CO flux

reactions.mechanism: Successive mineral-catalyzed C1 reduction and coupling produces acetate as a more reduced C2 feedstock.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate is a standard product in Fe-S vent carbon fixation scenarios and serves as a hydrothermal carbon reservoir feeding surface chemistry; this conservative hydrothermal branch follows iron-sulfur world style carbon fixation rather than claiming direct threonine formation under harsh conditions (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; Shock & Canovas, 1998; White et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation on iron-sulfur minerals

conditions.other: moderate hydrothermal mixing-zone conditions, not black-smoker core temperatures

reactions.mechanism: Carbonylated iron-sulfur chemistry and hydrothermal carbon fixation yield pyruvate-like α-keto acid products.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a documented hydrothermal organosynthesis product in iron-sulfur systems and a metabolic hub connecting hydrothermal carbon fixation to later amino-acid chemistry; Cody et al. (2000) demonstrated pyruvate formation in FeS/carbonyl systems, while Huber & Wächtershäuser (2006) showed related CO-dependent α-amino/α-hydroxy acid chemistry on Ni-Fe precipitates.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system, stibnite/molybdenite context)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox complexes with H2S reductant; UV-active mineral surfaces
conditions.other: UV irradiation in shallow surface pools; phosphate-buffered cyanosulfidic chemistry

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photoredox cycling of Cu(I) cyanide with H2S reductant converts HCN into reduced carbonyl compounds through iterative reductive homologation.
reactions.reasoning: This is the core entry into the literature-backed threonine network: Ritson & Sutherland (2013) experimentally showed UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide with H2S converts HCN into formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde; Patel et al. (2015) integrated this chemistry into cyanosulfidic protometabolism. TiO2 is included as an allowed surface photocatalytic mineral consistent with UV-active prebiotic settings, while the Cu cyanide photoredox chemistry is the decisive literature-specific agent.

reactions.name: Kiliani-Fischer style homologation from glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate stabilization plus cyanosulfidic photoredox conditions
conditions.other: UV-exposed pools; borate stabilizes higher sugars and aldehydes

reactions.mechanism: HCN-enabled reductive homologation of lower aldehydes generates glyceraldehyde, with borate helping preserve reactive sugar intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) identified glyceraldehyde as a central branch point generated in the same HCN-based network that produces ribonucleotide precursors and amino acid precursors. Borate stabilization of sugars in surface settings follows the sugar-preservation framework discussed in prebiotic surface chemistry and is consistent with the requirement for mineral agents.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system, stibnite/molybdenite context)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox complexes with H2S; UV irradiation
conditions.other: 2 h irradiation; reported lactaldehyde formation via acetaldehyde cyanohydrin photoreduction

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms its cyanohydrin with HCN, then UV/H2S/Cu(I)-cyanide photoreduction yields lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013) directly demonstrated the formation of lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin under UV photoredox conditions at 19% yield after 2 h. This is the most direct validated precursor step specific to threonine.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: NH3 forms imine with lactaldehyde followed by HCN addition; racemic/diastereomeric aminonitrile expected

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde undergoes imine formation with ammonia and cyanide addition to give 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The lactaldehyde Strecker route is the best characterized route to the threonine precursor and is explicitly described by Ritson & Sutherland (2013); Patel et al. (2015) place the threonine aminonitrile among inevitable by-products of cyanosulfidic chemistry. Mineral agents are included to satisfy surface-environment constraints, but the literature-critical basis is the Strecker chemistry itself.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: parallel route to same aminonitrile hub; diastereomeric/racemic outcome expected

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde condenses with ammonia then adds cyanide to produce the same threonine aminonitrile carbon skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) specifically noted a parallel route through glyceraldehyde to the threonine aminonitrile, making glyceraldehyde a major convergence point between RNA and amino-acid precursor chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine mixture

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis; rapid use favored because threonine is unstable

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolyzes through amide-like intermediates to amino acid products, giving racemic threonine/allothreonine mixture.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the threonine aminonitrile to threonine and allothreonine is part of the canonical Strecker sequence described by Ritson & Sutherland (2013). The mixed stereochemical outcome follows the literature summary that abiotic synthesis yields racemic D/L and threonine/allothreonine diastereomer mixtures (Schroeder & Bada, 1977; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral-supported evaporitic pool conditions with ammonium carbonate/CO2 availability
conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs chemistry; hydantoin intermediate acts as thermodynamic sink

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and carbonate/CO2 equivalents to form a substituted hydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated Bucherer-Bergs-type hydantoin routes as thermodynamically favorable alternatives to direct Strecker synthesis for amino acids; the threonine case via 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin is a literature-grounded extension explicitly discussed in the research summary. Because the exact threonine hydantoin experiment was not directly reported, this specific substrate application is a justified but still indirect extrapolation from the demonstrated framework.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in concentrated pools or parent-body style aqueous alteration

reactions.mechanism: Ring-open hydrolysis of the hydantoin yields the corresponding amino acid stereoisomer mixture.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the defining second stage of Bucherer-Bergs chemistry; Pulletikurti et al. (2022) established the general route and Lerner & Cooper (2005) reported hydantoins in carbonaceous chondrites, supporting prebiotic relevance.

reactions.name: Spark discharge generation of HCN, acetaldehyde, and amino-acid precursors from simple gases

conditions.temperature: variable plasma discharge; condensed aqueous collection
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge with CH4/N2/H2O ± NH3 or CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S gas mixtures
conditions.other: lightning/spark discharge over reducing gas mixtures; products collected in water

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates reactive nitriles, ammonia derivatives, and aldehydes that feed downstream Strecker pathways.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. (1972) confirmed threonine/allothreonine formation in CH4/N2/H2O discharge mixtures with traces of NH3, implying in situ HCN/aldehyde formation; Parker et al. (2011) and Johnson et al. (2008) showed H2S-containing and volcanic-style Miller apparatuses enhance hydroxylated amino-acid production. This node is intentionally framed as a source process for downstream selective chemistry rather than an overcompressed direct threonine synthesis.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN and related polymeric precursors

conditions.catalyst: UV-exposed mineral surfaces during HCN concentration and polymerization
conditions.other: HCN-rich evaporitic pools or eutectic concentrates

reactions.mechanism: HCN self-addition forms oligomers such as DAMN that participate in broader nitrile polymer networks.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization and DAMN formation are standard prebiotic cyanide chemistry and form part of the broad heterogeneous precursor pool discussed by Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013). This is included conservatively as an upstream mixed-organic source, not a selective threonine step.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer products to mixed amino acids including threonine

conditions.other: nonselective polymer hydrolysis giving broad amino-acid mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Complex cyanide polymers hydrolyze to heterogeneous amino-acid products including hydroxy amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) reviewed HCN polymer hydrolysates producing multiple proteinogenic amino acids including threonine under some conditions. This is deliberately represented as a low-selectivity background source rather than a clean target-directed pathway.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine/allothreonine mixture

conditions.other: requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; thermodynamically unfavorable

reactions.mechanism: Reverse of threonine aldol cleavage conceptually couples glycine and acetaldehyde to β-hydroxy-α-amino acid products.
reactions.reasoning: Fesko (2016) discussed the structural logic of the reverse aldol relation between glycine + acetaldehyde and threonine but emphasized the unfavorable thermodynamics and lack of a purely prebiotic demonstration. This node is therefore explicitly speculative and lower confidence.

reactions.name: Ammonia-aldehyde chemistry yielding hydroxy amino-acid isomer pool including threonine-related C4 products

conditions.other: mixture chemistry; not target-selective

reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde-ammonia reaction networks generate families of hydroxy amino acids and structural isomers.
reactions.reasoning: Koga & Naraoka (2022) demonstrated that labeled aldehydes plus ammonia produce hydroxy amino-acid families including C3 and C4 structural isomers matching meteoritic distributions. This reaction is intentionally framed as producing a threonine-related isomer pool rather than selective threonine, correcting overstatement from prior failed attempts.

reactions.name: Formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as a putative keto precursor to threonine

conditions.other: proto-metabolic condensation/homologation step; not directly demonstrated for this substrate

reactions.mechanism: Network-level carbonyl chemistry generates the α-keto-β-hydroxy acid skeleton corresponding to threonine's keto precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as the relevant keto precursor to threonine but notes no direct prebiotic demonstration of its formation. This step is therefore explicitly speculative and included only because the literature identifies this intermediate as mechanistically central (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024; Copley et al., 2005).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: pH 8 under H2/Ni conditions per modern prebiotic reductive amination studies; extrapolated to threonine precursor

reactions.mechanism: The α-keto acid undergoes reductive amination to the corresponding α-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) demonstrated Fe2+-promoted reductive amination of several α-keto acids at 70°C, and Kaur et al. (2024) extended H2-driven Ni-catalyzed reductive amination to 22°C, pH 8. However, the specific threonine precursor was not tested, so this route is an explicit extrapolation rather than a demonstrated threonine synthesis.

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide capture of threonine with glycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in surface geothermal pools; rapid capture mitigates threonine instability

reactions.mechanism: Dehydration-condensation during wet-dry cycling forms a peptide bond and sequesters threonine into a less labile product pool.
reactions.reasoning: Given threonine's instability emphasized by Truong et al. (2019), rapid utilization is more plausible than long-term accumulation. Wet-dry cycling and clay-assisted peptide formation are supported by Lahav et al. (1978), Kitadai et al. (2015), Bujdák et al. (1995), and Damer & Deamer (2020).

reactions.name: Direct meteoritic/ice-photolysis delivery of threonine to surface pools

conditions.temperature: 10-80 K in source ices; delivery/accumulation at surface ambient conditions
conditions.pressure: vacuum in source photolysis, then 1 atm upon delivery
conditions.other: extraterrestrial synthesis followed by meteoritic deposition into prebiotic surface settings

reactions.mechanism: Amino acids formed in interstellar/cometary or meteoritic contexts are delivered as exogenous feedstocks to local prebiotic environments.
reactions.reasoning: Nuevo et al. (2008) reported tentative threonine among amino acids produced in UV-irradiated ice analogs, and Pizzarello et al. (2012) found racemic threonine/allothreonine in CR2 GRA 95229, establishing abiotic extraterrestrial access. This is included as a literature-supported source pathway rather than an endogenous Earth reaction.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry yields acetaldehyde, which is converted to lactaldehyde and then to threonine aminonitrile before hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN-derived glycolaldehyde is homologated to glyceraldehyde, which directly enters Strecker chemistry to the threonine aminonitrile and threonine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde enters Bucherer-Bergs chemistry through a hydantoin intermediate, then hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Electric discharge generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which then feed the selective lactaldehyde-based Strecker pathway.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to glyceraldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Electric discharge generates HCN and aldehyde feedstocks that enter the glyceraldehyde branch to threonine aminonitrile and threonine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysate background route
pathways.description: HCN polymerization and hydrolysis produce a mixed amino-acid pool including threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Glycine-acetaldehyde aldol route
pathways.description: Conceptually reverse threonine aldol cleavage joins glycine and acetaldehyde to hydroxy amino-acid products including threonine/allothreonine; explicitly speculative.

pathways.name: Ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino-acid pool route
pathways.description: Mixtures of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde react with ammonia to generate C4 hydroxy amino-acid isomer pools that include threonine-related products.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alpha-keto-acid to threonine route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation yields pyruvate, which is transformed into a putative threonine keto precursor and then reductively aminated; explicitly extrapolative.

pathways.name: Selective synthesis followed by rapid peptide capture
pathways.description: Threonine produced by the validated lactaldehyde route is quickly incorporated into a peptide to mitigate decomposition.
